topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien in my hands, heavy with an authority I didn't possess. I traced the elegant script detailing my life—the triumphs, the quiet moments, the supposed struggles. Every sentence was a carefully curated portrait, and the central figure in every narrative was, undeniably, me. But the dates, the anecdotes about my childhood, the very phrasing of my ambitions—it all felt borrowed. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as I realized the meticulous construction of this biography hadn't sprung from my own recollections. A sharp, almost painful tightening spread across my chest, and I quickly slammed the folder shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet study. I needed to know who had penned this, and why they felt entitled to my history.

[story 2]

I reread the final chapter, my gaze snagging on a passage describing a pivotal decision I’d made years ago. The description was flawless, imbued with a depth of understanding I hadn't felt myself. A simmering heat rose in my neck, and I leaned back in my chair, my fingers gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles bleached white. This wasn't just inaccurate; it was a complete appropriation of my narrative. I started pacing the length of the room, my steps quick and agitated. "Who authorized this?" I demanded of the empty air, my voice tight with a brittle edge. The silence offered no comfort, only the heavy weight of being overshadowed by someone else's version of reality.

[story 3]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow when I saw the footnotes—subtle, almost invisible markers pointing to sources I had never encountered. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. A profound sense of unfairness washed over me, settling deep in my bones like ice water. I picked up the manuscript again, my hands trembling slightly. I scrutinized the prose, searching for the fingerprint of the author, the deliberate choices that shaped this external version of me. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I began to cross-reference the timelines, my movements becoming jerky and obsessive. Every detail felt scrutinized under a harsh, unforgiving light, and the air in the room seemed too thin to breathe.
